context:
## Dieses Dokument

- Titel: Memorandum für Beverly Baxter
- Signatur: SZ-AP2/W-H62a
- Datum: 4. 1. 40
- Sprache: Englisch
- Objekttyp: Typoskriptdurchschlag
- Umfang: 5 Blatt
- Hand: Lotte Zweig

pages.transcription:
Memorandum für Beverly Baxter
4. I 40

As an Austrian who since his childhood has been
familiar with the relations between the former Austria and
Italy, I would like to draw the attention to an actual point
in the present political constellation.

The relations between the old Austria and Italy
have always been insincere owing to the demand of Italy for
"Trento e Trieste", the country of Trentino and the port of
Trieste. A large group in the Austrian Ministeries was
willing, for the sake of the security of prewar Austria,
to sacrifice the Trentino, but all parties were united in
the strict refusal to give up Trieste. Trieste was the
harbour of the fifty million central-European Empire of
Austria, and it was essential for its commerce in the
Mediterranean and with the East.

Now, that Germany has taken over Austria and
Czechoslovakia, there can be no doubt that sooner or later
they will ask for this port which belonged to the old
Austria, and for their commerce an outlet to the Medi-
terranean is absolutely necessary for a country of a
hundred million inhabitants. It is even a hundred times
more important than Danzig, and Germany has only deferred
this demand according to its principle always to attack
pages.notes: Handschriftliche Notiz am Kopf der Seite.

pages.transcription:
- 2 -

the weakest point first. But one day - and especially
if Germany should come out of this war with some strength -
it will and must make this demand for Trieste.

This fact is, of course, well known in Italy.
It is a mistake that the newspapers always point out that
Italy is afraid of the preponderance of Germany in Hungary
or on the Balkan. The real fear in the informed circles is
that a Germany which reaches from the North Sea until a few
hundred miles from the Adriatic, will force its way through
to the Adriatic Sea. Trieste is the sceleton in the house
of the German-Italian relations not less - and even more -
than it was the sceleton in the time of the Triple Alliance
between Austria and Italy.

That this is true is proved by the fact that never
the word Trieste has been mentioned in the German-Italian
relations. No one in Italy talks about this possibility
and yet everybody in responsible circles is strongly con-
cerned about it. It seems, therefore, of utmost importance
that just now this word is brought into the limelight of
discussion and in a way that it cannot be suppressed.
A mere mentioning of the problem of Trieste in newspaper
articles would be without value, for these articles could
easily be suppressed by the German and Italian censorship.
This undeniable fact of the German aspiration for Trieste

- 3 -

which is only deferred, should be pointed out in such a
way that its discussion cannot be suppressed, for instance
in a speech of the leading statesmen such as Chamberlain
or Lord Halifax which go all over the world and cannot be
suppressed.

I do not overlook, of course, that such a
mentioning must be made in such a way that it does not
seem to be made in the intention of driving a wedge
between Germany and Italy. It should, therefore, not only
be spoken of Trieste but in connection with all other
possible demands which a victorious Germany would raise
with the argumentation of the necessary "Lebensraum".
It could for instance be said that England, as long as
Germany did only ask for the return of the German mino-
rities in other countries, has always shown under-
standing for this wish of Germany and has not resisted
it in the hope that in this way a peaceful reorganisation
of Europe would be furthered. But hardly had Germany
reached its aim when it produced a new thesis, that of
the "living space" which in advance justifies every act
of agression. Which demands can be contained in this
elastic word "Lebensraum", is widely known by the
writings of the "Alldeutschen Verband", by the theory

- 4 -

of geopolitics of professor Haushofer and by the maps
which are again and again drawn in Germany since 1914.
One day the Dutch mouth of the Rhine should be given up
to Germany because the Rhine is a German stream and
Holland therefore belongs to the German "Lebensraum",
another time it will have to be Trieste, the harbour of
old Austria, which should be returned to Germany because
Germany has the right to all former Austrian territory -
and because Trieste is indispensable as the accesion to
the Mediterranean for its trade with the Orient. Just
as well the Black Sea belongs to its sphere of domination
because the Danube has its origin in Germany and Austria.
The Stebenbürger Sachsen will have to be freed from the
yoke of Hungary or Rumania etcetera, etcetera. That the
whole of Europa belongs to the sphere of the German "Le-
bensraum", in this theory all the theoreticians of National
Socialism are united, and their discussions only refer to
which aim - either the Mediterranean, the North Sea, the
Mouth of the Rhine, the Balkan - should be attacked first.

I only give these instances as one of the many
possibilities how the word Trieste and the German demand
for this former German harbour could publicly be mentioned.
Perhaps there is a better way of doing this. But in any

- 5 -

case I am convinced that it is necessary to bring Trieste
into the discussion just in the present moment. It is
quite likely that any such intention will immediately be
denied by Germany, but such a dementi would only serve
further to draw the attention of the wide public in Italy
to this problem. Tendencious lies have no strength.
But what would give its effect to a discussion about
Trieste is the fact that in reality Trieste is an actual
and even necessary object in the German Imperialism,
and the reason for a future inevitable conflict between
Italy, which cannot tolerate Germany in the Mediterranean
and Germany which will and must gain access to the Me-
diterranean

terranean is absolutely necessary for a country of a
hundred million inhabitants. It is even a hundred times

result.confidence_notes: Die Transkription ist aufgrund der klaren Maschinenschrift sehr sicher. Die handschriftlichen Notizen auf der ersten Seite sind gut lesbar.
